# Questions in Livestream 219

Live Stream #219: Ten Things You Wish You Knew

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=470" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:50</a> | What do you think about novels including a bibliography or sources list when they interact with real texts? | He likes the idea as a way to separate research from invention and help readers follow what informed the novel. | He is fine with a bibliography at the end of a novel because it functions like supplemental material and does not interrupt the story. He distinguishes that from footnotes, which he dislikes in fiction because they break the reader's flow, even though he wants abundant footnotes in nonfiction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:50</a> | Do some early-Exodus advocates hold that view because "older equals better"? | Sometimes yes; he warns that "older is better" can be a fallacy if it replaces evidence. | He identifies this as an argument from antiquity or tradition and says some early-date supporters really do think their view goes back to the apostles. He argues that the early Exodus date as a modern position only arose around 1906, while the late-date view is actually older by several decades, so age alone cannot settle the question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=845" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:05</a> | Why weren't foreign slaves freed in the Jubilee? | Because Jubilee restored Israelite land and kinship holdings rather than abolishing every kind of servitude. | He reads Leviticus 25 and frames the issue around land, clan membership, and support systems. Israelite debt servants could return to family land at release, but foreign slaves had no inherited land or clan support, so releasing them without a place to live or work would leave them destitute. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=1089" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:09</a> | Could foreigners eventually become Israelites or join a clan? | Yes, through incorporation and assimilation over time rather than by instantly erasing lineage distinctions. | He says a non-Israelite could be incorporated into a clan, especially through marriage into a family where inheritance passed through daughters. That incorporation would still work through the wife's inheritance and children, and he distinguishes it from laws about entry into the assembly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=1186" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:46</a> | Is Mosaic law arranged so the nation's good comes before tribe, clan, family, and individual? | Broadly yes; he says the law often prioritizes covenantal and national order over modern individualism. | He actually nuances the proposed hierarchy by saying Mosaic law is usually clan-first rather than nation-first. In his description, clan integrity receives priority, then the individual is cared for within that structure, and the nation benefits from the stability of those smaller units. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=1301" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:41</a> | If dragons correspond to any known living creatures, what would they be? | He treats dragon traditions as composite and mythic, drawing on real animal impressions rather than one exact living match. | He says dragons need not be living animals at all; they might be large reptiles, chaos monsters, forces of nature, fears, or other spiritual imagery. He stresses that people can recognize the concept of a dragon more easily than define it, so no single modern creature should be treated as the answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=1531" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:31</a> | Did dinosaur bones help inspire dragon traditions? | Possibly in some cases, though he does not think that explains every dragon story. | He says fossil finds could have contributed to some dragon traditions and mentions the idea of intrusion fossils, where fossils move out of their original strata. But he also cautions that alleged temple fossil evidence is uncertain and may have been moved or dumped later, so the answer remains possible rather than proven. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=1820" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:20</a> | Do you have a favorite word? | Yes: "propinquity," which he likes both for its meaning of nearness and for the way it sounds. | He defines "propinquity" as nearness in place or time and says he likes both the meaning and the sound of the word. The attraction is partly semantic and partly auditory, because he enjoys the ringing quality of the word itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=1921" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:01</a> | Since receiving your doctorate, have you ever had a "trust me, I'm a doctor" moment? | Not quite, though he says he came close once when speaking as a newly minted expert at a school event. | He tells a story from just after his PhD when Liverpool sent him to speak at a primary school about ancient Egypt. When the teacher introduced him by first name, he reflexively corrected it to "Dr. Falk," which he says was the closest he came to that sort of moment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=2127" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:27</a> | Have you now seen four decades of technological change, and has that been good overall? | Yes. He says the movement from typewriters to AI has been good overall, even if not perfect. | He says he has seen technology move from typewriters to AI and considers the overall change good. He adds examples from his own background, including working near SGML and HTML development, knowing people involved in standards work, and writing boot code for a major supercomputer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=2788" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:28</a> | What is the most significant technology of the last hundred years? | Pharmaceuticals, especially antibiotics and vaccines, because of how many lives and diseases they have affected. | He argues that pharmaceuticals affected more people than computers, with antibiotics, vaccines, and disease treatments as the most revolutionary changes. He uses polio and the Salk vaccine as an example, while also noting that pharmaceutical safety and efficacy testing require regulation and make development expensive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=2975" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:35</a> | Does forgiveness mean the absence of consequences? | No. He says forgiveness belongs to the offended party, but consequences can still remain. | He distinguishes forgiveness from the removal of every result of wrongdoing. A victim may forgive, but the law, boundaries, restitution, or natural consequences can still remain, as in the example of theft where the offended person forgives but the state still has an interest in justice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=3196" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:16</a> | How long do burned discs like CDs and DVDs survive? | He says it depends on the medium, ranging from a few years to many decades, with better discs often averaging around fifty years. | He says burned disc life depends heavily on disc type and quality, with a rough range from a couple of years to about a century. Better data-grade discs may average around fifty years, while cheap discs and media with poor dyes, seals, or glue can fail much sooner through disc rot. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=3232" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:52</a> | Is infant baptism the historical position? | He says the history is real but complicated and not something that resolves into a simple one-line answer. | He treats the history as complicated rather than a simple yes. He notes that early references can involve different concepts, including "baptism in blood" as martyrdom, and that many early Christians delayed baptism because of beliefs about post-baptismal sin, so the evidence cannot be flattened into a simple historical slogan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=4264" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:04</a> | Did the apostles baptize whole households including babies and young children? | Possibly, but not necessarily; he notes that Roman households did not always include small children living at home. | He says household baptisms could have included infants, but the evidence is thin and not something he would stake a claim on. Roman households did not always include small children, and even a whole-household conversion under the father's authority would not automatically prove individual infant faith or establish a norm. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=4789" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:49</a> | In Isaiah 66:8, what does "nation" mean? | He says the word there means a Gentile nation and uses the contrast to highlight God bringing Israel forth in a day. | He says the word refers to a Gentile or foreign nation and functions in prophetic poetic contrast. Ordinary nations form over time, but the passage pictures Zion bringing forth her sons at once, highlighting God creating Israel as a nation in a day. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=4935" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:15</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have pets like we do? | Yes. He says Egyptians especially kept cats, while elites also kept dogs such as greyhounds. | He says Egyptians did have pets, especially cats, and kings could have dogs such as greyhounds. He adds that most ordinary cats were less pampered than modern pets: they hunted mice, rats, and birds, and some were trained in ways comparable to working hunting animals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=5184" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:24</a> | What do you think of Justin Martyr and his writings? | He says Justin is worth reading, but like the other church fathers, he is a mixed case whose mileage may vary. | He says he has read Justin Martyr, though not recently, and treats him like other church fathers: useful but not uniformly right. He encourages reading many fathers rather than one, because they agree on core Christian claims but vary widely on secondary issues and should be evaluated case by case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=5587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:07</a> | When is the cutoff for the church fathers? | He says there is no single cutoff; some stop around Nicaea or the seventh century, while others extend the category much later. | He explains that some distinguish ante-Nicene and post-Nicene fathers, while later cutoffs vary by tradition. In the West the category often ends around the East-West Schism, but Eastern traditions may count later figures such as John of Damascus, so the boundary is traditional rather than exact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=5830" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:10</a> | What is your opinion of InspiringPhilosophy? | Very positive. He says he has even co-written some material with him and respects the way he improved earlier work after criticism. | He says he has co-written some material with InspiringPhilosophy and highly recommends much of his work. He respects that after Falk criticized an earlier video, IP listened, retracted it, and remade the material, though Falk still does not recommend every single video. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=5944" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:04</a> | Is Mike Jones Anglican, Lutheran, or Presbyterian? | He says Mike Jones has not said and keeps that private, as Falk also does, because naming a denomination can create division or be mistaken for endorsement. | He says Mike Jones has not revealed his denomination, and Falk keeps his own private for similar reasons. Naming it can create tribal division, be mistaken for an unqualified endorsement, or cause criticism of one's own denomination to be misunderstood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=6348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:48</a> | Any recommendations for studying Romans? | He recommends starting with a good commentary rather than relying on a light series alone. | He recommends starting with a strong commentary for a serious study of Romans and mentions the Word Biblical Commentary and NICNT/NIC series as good places to begin. He does not point to a specific personal series of his own for Romans. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=6441" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:21</a> | Has Khirbet Qeiyafa been confirmed as one of the main proposed sites? | No. He says the identification remains uncertain. | He says the site identification is not certain and could fit more than one proposed biblical name. The evidence is not enough to decide the matter, and the ostracon has not settled it because its readings and translations vary widely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=6506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:26</a> | Was Khirbet Qeiyafa abandoned after the United Monarchy because the Philistine threat faded? | That is one possible explanation, but he says it is not the only one. | He says Khirbet Qeiyafa was occupied in Iron Age IIA, during the United Monarchy period, and seems to have been abandoned sometime afterward before later reoccupation. A reduced Philistine threat could explain that, but he also suggests the small fortified site might simply have been absorbed by something larger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=6573" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:33</a> | Was Siamun the Pharaoh who destroyed Gezer and gave it to Solomon? | Probably yes; he says Siamun is the best candidate by far. | He says Siamun is the best candidate by a long way because his reign overlaps well with Solomon, though Psusennes II is also possible. He rules out Shoshenq I because that later pharaoh is a rival in the biblical setting, and the text does not specify exactly when in Solomon's reign the Gezer episode occurred. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=6858" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:18</a> | Are there ancient records of exorcism-type practices being effective before Jesus? | We have records of exorcisms, but not solid records proving whether they worked. | He says ancient sources record exorcism practices in Second Temple, apocryphal, Mesopotamian, and Egyptian contexts, but they do not securely establish efficacy. Christian writers later argue that Christian exorcisms were effective, but records of attempted exorcisms are easier to find than records proving success. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=7582" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:22</a> | Did the slave girl Paul exorcised want to be freed? | He says she was not resisting; the real resistance came from her owners. | He says the slave girl herself is not portrayed as resisting the exorcism. The resistance came from her masters, who did not want her freed because the spirit was profitable for them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=7684" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:04</a> | Is it prayer and fasting, or mostly prayer, that matters in those exorcism passages? | He treats fasting as intensified prayer and says prayer is the main thing shaping the heart toward God. | He describes fasting as accelerated or focused prayer: it does not change God, but it changes and aligns the person praying. Fasting without prayer merely makes someone hungry, while fasting joined to prayer concentrates the heart and attention on God's purposes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=8050" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:10</a> | Would an exclusively digital Bible translation that gets updated over time be acceptable? | Yes, in principle, as long as the textual base and licensing are handled carefully. | He says there is nothing wrong in principle with a digital translation that improves over time, but the starting text matters. Unless it is translated from scratch, the project needs an out-of-copyright or properly licensed revision base, such as the ASV or another text with clear permissions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=8207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:47</a> | Do you think at least sixty Negev fortifications belong to David's reign? | He treats the idea as worth exploring from the biblical data, but not as something he proves as a slam-dunk just from the question itself. | He says the number is not unreasonable if many of the sites are very small fortifications rather than large cities. He references Ralph Hawkins's work and describes some Davidic fortifications as tiny circular forts with a few rooms, a wall, and perhaps a watchtower. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=8451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:51</a> | What do you think of the Jurassic Park series, and do you have a favorite dinosaur? | He enjoys the series overall and names Spinosaurus as his favorite dinosaur. | He says he liked the first three movies and the first book, noting that the novel differs sharply from the film even when familiar dialogue appears. Later in the discussion he names Spinosaurus as his favorite dinosaur because it is interesting and unusual. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=8978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:38</a> | Do you think there will ever be a schism in ABR between 1446 and 1406 early-Exodus camps? | No. He does not expect that dating difference to create a major institutional split. | He does not expect a schism; he expects ABR to drift toward the 1406 position as younger scholars and Septuagint-priority arguments gain influence. He thinks the 1446 group will fade rather than split the institution, while both sides remain within an early-Exodus framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=9204" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:24</a> | Are some intelligent-design proponents chasing a hidden "God code" in nature or DNA? | Yes, sometimes; he says you really do see that tendency in parts of the movement. | He says some intelligent-design proponents do seek something like a "God code" in DNA, comparable to hidden-message or Bible-code thinking. He does not apply that to all design advocates and says there are better design arguments than hidden-sequence searches. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=9412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:52</a> | What is your recording and production process for music? | He says he handles the recording and production himself and then sketches the basic workflow he uses. | He says he composes in MuseScore, adds sound fonts, processes the output through a DAC, and tweaks the result himself. He writes the score and instrumentation, then records from that software-driven output rather than using a separate studio workflow. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=9609" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:09</a> | How would you lead, structure, and educate a church? | He would build leadership and structure through deep teaching, then recognize elders as people visibly rise to that role. | He ties leadership, structure, and education together: a pastor should teach in a way that raises up elders, and elders should be able to teach and eventually raise up others. In his model, mature elders become pastors, new churches form, and church growth comes through discipleship rather than mere administration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=9868" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:28</a> | Can you explain the Cain-line and Seth-line view of Genesis 6 versus the other interpretations? | He explains it as a human-lineage reading of Genesis 6 and contrasts it with the angelic reading without treating the text as simple. | He explains the Cain/Seth view as the old 20th-century idea that the "sons of God" are Seth's righteous line and the "daughters of men" are Cain's line. He says that view has fallen out of favor because the text does not directly connect those lines to the terms, and he also critiques angelic-Nephilim readings as heavily debated and problematic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=10458" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:18</a> | Are demons fallen angels or of some other origin? | Both. He says some are fallen angels and some are other kinds of spirit beings. | He says demons include both fallen angels and other kinds of spirit beings, but he rejects deriving them from human procreation. He describes a layered spirit world with multiple created orders, including chaos monsters, angelic beings, and embodied humans, rather than reducing all demons to one origin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=10893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:33</a> | Are the extra books in the Ethiopian Bible canon? | No. He says the Ethiopian Bible has an expansive apocrypha, not extra books that he would treat as canon. | He says the Ethiopian Bible contains an expansive apocrypha and pseudepigrapha, among the largest such collections in Christian Bibles, but outside the Ethiopian Orthodox Church those books are not treated as canon by most Christians. He warns against a pastor seeking revelation from those books instead of feeding people from recognized Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=11919" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:39</a> | The Assyrian Church of the East denies being Nestorian. Thoughts? | He says much of the dispute involved sloppy historical wording and that, if the church does not actually hold the condemned view, the old label should not control everything. | He thinks the separation involved accusations of Nestorianism, but the Assyrian Church of the East would describe the problem as sloppy wording and misunderstanding. He compares this to Coptic disputes over monophysitism and says inherited labels should be reconsidered if the church does not actually teach the condemned position. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=12048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:48</a> | Does the Coptic Orthodox Church still care about old theological disputes, given oppression by Muslims? | He says not much; if the issue was sloppy wording and the church does not hold the condemned view now, he sees little practical dispute left. | He says the Coptic Orthodox Church largely does not care about those old disputes in the same way now and would shrug at the charge if it was based on bad wording. If Copts are not actually monophysite and the Assyrian Church is not actually Nestorian, he sees little remaining issue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=12111" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:51</a> | Are ABD positions worth considering for current ABDs? | No. His blunt advice is not to stay ABD if you can help it. | He says ABD is not a degree and should not be treated like one, because the dissertation is the doctorate. He allows that an ABD-only job can be worth applying for if it is just a job requirement, but his main advice is to finish quickly and avoid lingering in dissertation delay. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=12578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:38</a> | Where can I read more about the claim that biblical law often states the harshest punishment while allowing lesser resolutions? | He points to recent books on biblical law and broader ancient Near Eastern law as the place to start. | He says recent books on biblical law and ancient Mediterranean or Middle Eastern law codes are the place to look, especially discussions of Mosaic law as descriptive or case law. He also points to biblical examples where a harsh penalty exists but a fine or other resolution can settle the matter. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=12701" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:41</a> | Any knowledge about a BC, Alberta, and Saskatchewan merger or separation from Canada? | He says Alberta has made some moves in that direction, while the broader regional picture is still unsettled. | He says Alberta has moved somewhat in that direction and Saskatchewan has started a process, but British Columbia is not involved and is too politically different to join such an alliance. He therefore rejects the idea of a BC-Alberta-Saskatchewan merger as stated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=13022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:02</a> | What do you think of Thomas Schmidt's Josephus commentary or testimonium work? | He says he would need to look into it before giving a real opinion. | He gives no substantive evaluation and says he would have to look into Thomas Schmidt's Josephus commentary or testimonium work before answering. The transcript supports only a limited answer here. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=13037" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:17</a> | Would Adam and Eve have used carved bone needles for sewing? | Probably yes; he thinks carved bone, and perhaps wood, fits better than metal needles that early. | He says they probably would have used carved bone and sinew, and he also allows wood as possible though less ideal. He jokes that fig leaves did not go well, then returns to the practical point that carved bone would likely work better than wood for sewing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=13113" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:33</a> | Since there is no marriage in heaven, what might our relationship with our spouse be like there? | He expects deep friendship rather than the present form of marriage. | He expects spouses who had a good marriage to remain close friends, describing them as "besties" without the present physical aspect of marriage. He frames marriage as more than physical relationship: a friendship that begins now and can continue into eternity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=13357" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:37</a> | Why did Horemheb apparently have no sons to succeed him? | He says we know too little to be certain beyond the fact that the expected male heir never materialized. | He says Horemheb was married, or at least had someone filling the ritual role of queen, but he appears not to have produced surviving children. Falk cannot say why, though he allows possibilities such as children dying young or being removed, and stresses that the evidence only lets us say he had no known successor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/m5tH6mEi86w?t=13680" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:00</a> | What are the top three early church fathers to start with? | He especially recommends First Clement, then points readers to figures like Ignatius, Polycarp, and Papias. | He first recommends First Clement, then says Ignatius, Polycarp, Papias, Irenaeus, Tertullian, and Hippolytus are all useful options. When pushed toward three, he leans toward First Clement, Irenaeus, and Tertullian, with Athanasius as a strong later fourth choice and advice to read sequentially rather than cherry-pick. |
